content:
I understand your frustration behind the ranking system, especially after the real-time contest when climbing leagues becomes much harder.

However, the puzzle statement currently reads as if the described behaviour reflects the real arena ranking system. Since the real system uses TrueSkill (link 1) and handles draws in a variable way (link 2), it could be misleading for players who only see the statement and not your comment here that explains the intent.

Please revise the statement so it clearly frames the three rules as simplified or hypothetical models inspired by the arena, rather than implying they describe the actual ranking mechanism or an actual committee that decides the mechanism.

You may also want to check the official explanations of the ranking system and draw handling for reference:
• https://forum.codingame.com/t/multiplayer-contests-ranking-system/381/6
• https://forum.codingame.com/t/ultimate-tic-tac-toe-puzzle-discussion/22616/13

Also, I know that the real CG scoring system is slightly more complex. This problem reduces its complexity but still clearly demonstrates why handling draws properly might be the key to correct and STABLE ratings. Most of you know how much harder is to beat Silver/Gold league after ending of the real-time contest. It's mostly because of the draws. So the problem is like, you know, some meta-irony on this order of things. Don't know really, is it needed to inverse the loops order to show that the LATEST player will gain worst score on draws or it is obvious by itself.

Question: is it hard or medium? The solution is not very tricky. But needs some understanding of underlying problem as I think.
UPD: updated description to be crystal clear. Now the question is it easy or medium? OR maybe it is okay for Clash Of Code?

Thank you for the feedback! Yes, I agree that the task description could use some refinement - although I'd like to keep a touch of irony, it's not right to mislead users. CG doesn't actually use exactly this rating algorithm, though I'll note that, overall, among the three options, it's the closest to what I referred to as "CG-Style."
Regarding the links: TrueSkill is primarily optimized not for rating precision but for convergence speed. And while it would be interesting to implement it in this task (by selecting the 10 random opponents from the 10th, 20th, 30th percentiles, etc, and then gradually reducing the uncertainty factor), it would be significantly harder to design deterministic test cases for it. Still, I'll give it some thought.
In completed contests, I've observed that the final rating doesn't match (link 1), and no additional matches are played - even upon reaching the top 15 on the arenas I'm familiar with (okay, granted, that's not all arenas, just a few). Moreover, regardless of rating or uncertainty, players often end up with a fixed number of games: 70, 100, or 150. Draws, too, are usually not treated as draws on most (some?) arenas (f.e. Crystal Rush I've played recently) - though that's likely on the game developers' side. Yet the rating still fluctuates, and, to reiterate, it follows rules very similar to those in the CG-Style algorithm. Ultimate Tic-Tac-Toe (discussion in link 2) (well, I've played it too) might be one of the exceptions, though.
That said, I see you agree that frustration is understandable here: in finished contests, even if you consistently beat level boss bots at a 60+% win rate, you can even still fail to break into the top 100 league players due to the nuances described above.

Thank you for the detailed reply.

> while it would be interesting to implement it (TrueSkill) in this task… it would be significantly harder to design deterministic test cases for it.

Just to clarify, I never suggested that the puzzle should implement the real arena system (TrueSkill).

> In completed contests, I've observed that the final rating doesn't match (link 1), and no additional matches are played - even upon reaching the top 15 on the arenas I'm familiar with

I'm aware that some operational details have evolved over time (for example, the exact way post-contest matches are rerun). However, reruns did occur in both contests you participated in. The other arenas where you reached "top 15" are *not* contests but ongoing games, so reruns don't apply there. In those arenas, matches continue to occur over time whenever you or other participants submit new code (and your bot is chosen in the latter case). 

> players often end up with a fixed number of games: 70, 100, or 150

When you submit your code, your bot is initially evaluated by running a fixed batch of games. After that, it may still participate in additional matches from time to time as I mentioned above, which is why the rating can continue to fluctuate.

Finally, I just want to reiterate my original suggestion: it would help to clarify in the puzzle statement that the three scoring rules are hypothetical models inspired by arena behaviour, rather than implying that they correspond directly to the real ranking algorithm. It would also probably be better not to introduce something like a "League Committee" in a way that sounds like an official part of the platform.

Thanks again.

Thank you. Updated description.

I'm not going to argue about the rating system; I think I need to gather more information first.

Thanks for the update.

Just to clarify, I wasn't trying to argue about the rating system (or anything else, for that matter) in my earlier replies.

Below are my comments on the puzzle:

Goal
* Please replace both instances of "systems" with "rules" for consistency (the statement mentions "three different rules"). Also, the algorithm itself is the same and only the draw handling differs, so it's one system with three rules rather than three separate systems.

* Please change "Draws split" to "Draw splits" so the wording matches the syntax used in Neutral and Cruel.

* The algorithm specifies [[K]] = 2.0, but in the Neutral rule draws effectively use [[K]] = 0.0 (ratings are not updated). Please either mention this explicitly, or state that ratings are not updated for draws, under the Algorithm section. (The current wording "For each match, update ratings immediately…" does not indicate this exception.)

* [[R_i]] and [[R_j]] appear both in the left-hand side and right-hand side of the formulas, so they refer to both the previous and the updated ratings. Please clarify this instead of describing them as "current ratings".

* Please change "[[N-1]]" to "[[N]]-1" (twice).

* Please change "[[i+1]]" to "[[i]]+1".

* Consider changing "correspondingly" to "respectively".

Input
* The explanations for values 1 and -1 currently refer to i and j, but since this part describes entries of the matrix rather than a specific processed pair, it's clearer to say that [[M[i][j]]] represents the result from bot [[i]]'s perspective.

Constraints
* Please change "System" to "Algorithm".

Tags
* Please remove the tag “Numerical methods” and try to use existing tags instead.

* Please remove the tag "Logical problem" as it doesn't seem very relevant.

* A more relevant tag here would be "Simulation".

Cases
* I understand that you're still working on the cases. Just a gentle reminder that each test and its corresponding validator should cover a similar scenario with slightly different inputs and outputs (where possible). For example, there are matches between four bots in Test 1, but there are no matches in Validator 1 because it only contains one bot. Since these represent very different situations, this pair of cases should be revised to align them better. You may also split the pair into two pairs of cases.

* It's also better to make Test 1 a simple but representative case, since it appears in the Example section. In this puzzle, that would mean using a matrix that includes all possible values (1, -1, 0).

Thanks again.

Additional comments (not a request to change the puzzle)
* Just as an observation: the puzzle assumes that when two bots play, their order is fixed. Under the Cruel rule this naturally introduces bias because draws always favour the second player. On the platform, matches are usually played in both directions, which tends to mitigate order-related bias.

* The puzzle also assumes a fixed outcome for each pair of bots. In real arenas different configurations (maps, starting positions, etc.) can lead to different outcomes even for the same pair of bots.

* So while the puzzle highlights how certain rules can produce unfair results, those additional factors in practice can reduce the effect.
